title: Polgar Tactics Sampler
subtitle: Three sharp tournament positions
summary: Solve a small set of tactical positions from the Polgar sisters games, with original prompts and explanations.
attribution: Factual chess positions from Laszlo Polgar, Chess: 5334 Problems, Combinations and Games, Polgar sisters tournament-games combinations. Original prompts and feedback.

steps.title: Calculate forcing moves
steps.markdown: These positions come from the Polgar sisters tournament-games section of the tactics collection. The prompts are new, but the training habit is classic: start with forcing moves: [[check|checks]], [[capture|captures]], and direct threats.

steps.title: Use the open file
steps.markdown: White's rook on a1 has a clear road. Find the move that invades and overloads Black's defense.

hints.text: Look for a rook move all the way to the back rank.

feedback.correct: Correct. The rook enters on a8 and Black's pieces are tied down.
feedback.wrong: Start with the rook that has a clear file.

steps.title: Hit the loose piece
steps.markdown: Black's queen is active, but one nearby defender is loose. Find White's clean capture.

hints.text: Check which black piece on the fourth rank is undefended.
The b-file rook can climb to b4.

feedback.correct: Good. Rxb4 removes the defender and leaves Black's queen short of good squares.
feedback.wrong: Look for a rook capture on the b-file.

steps.title: Open the king
steps.markdown: White has more pieces near the king. Find the forcing capture that starts the attack.

hints.text: Look at every capture near Black's king.
The queen can take on h6.

feedback.correct: Yes. Qxh6+ pulls open the king's shelter and keeps the attack moving.
feedback.wrong: The key move is a queen capture with check.

steps.title: The pattern
steps.markdown: In each position, the move was forcing: a rook invasion, a capture, or a check near the king. That is the point of tactics training: make the most forcing candidates visible first.
